value.Title: Donald Trump endorses Jackson Lahmeyer in key Oklahoma primary race
value.Overview: The Daily Oklahoman Online (Oklahoma), 601words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHN-80B3-RTPK-749C-00000-00Donald Trump endorses Jackson Lahmeyer in key Oklahoma primary race2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:00ZLexisNexisDonald Trump endorses Jackson Lahmeyer in key Oklahoma primary raceAlex Gladden, The Oklahoman

President Donald Trump is supporting a prominent Tulsa pastor in the highly contested race to represent Oklahoma's 1st Congressional District.

Trump endorsed Jackson Lahmeyer over 10 other Republican candidates. In a state where Oklahomans have voted for the president in every county in each of Trump's three bids for commander in chief, Trump's endorsement can make a campaign.

“It’s the honor of a lifetime to have the endorsement of President Trump, who I’ve always said is the best president in my lifetime," Lahmeyer said.

More: The biggest surprises from election filings in Oklahoma

Lahmeyer is seeking to replace current U.S. Rep. Kevin Hern, who is running for the state's open U.S. Senate position.

“This catapults us into the position to win," Lahmeyer said about the endorsement, which Trump made in a Truth Social post on Thursday, March 7.

More: In Oklahoma governor's race, candidates spend millions more than they raise

Even before the official endorsement, political insiders suspected Trump would endorse Lahmeyer. Lahmeyer is associated with Rudy Giuliani and others in Trump's circle, and the president's sons Eric and Donald Jr. have attended Sheridan Church, where Lahmeyer preaches, multiple times.

Experts have aligned Lahmeyer with the growing Christian Nationalist movement. He has voiced a desire to insert Christianity into areas of government, including schools.

Lahmeyer was unsuccessful in his bid to unseat U.S. Sen. James Lankford in 2022, but he said that he has been building on that momentum in the meantime.

“You know when the president makes an endorsement, he doesn’t lose," Lahmeyer said, before pointing to Trump's record in May 5 Indiana primaries, when nearly all of his picks won.

Link to Image

Trump singled out Lahmeyer as a "MAGA warrior" who founded Pastors For Trump.

"Jackson Lahmeyer has my complete and total endorsement to be the next representative from Oklahoma’s 1st Congressional District — HE WILL NEVER LET YOU DOWN!" Trump said in the Truth Social post.

Lahmeyer described his priorities as America-first and Oklahoma-first. He said keeping the nation's border secure is important to him, as well as ensuring the Chinese Communist Party does not own Oklahoma farmland. The news outlet Investigate Midwest found that renewable energy companies from Canada and Europe own a majority of the foreign-owned farmland in the state.

Lahmeyer has not yet reported any campaign donations to the Federal Election Commission. He said in March that he had raised more than $100,000 for the race, without disclosing the identities of his donors.

On Friday, May 8, he launched an ad touting Trump's endorsement of his campaign.

Previous Coverage Trump ally Jackson Lahmeyer seeks Oklahoma congress seat

Kim David, the chairwoman of the state Corporation Commission, and Rep. Mark Tedford, R-Tulsa, are other influential candidates competing against Lahmeyer for the seat.

In light of the president's endorsement of Lahmeyer, Tedford said nothing about his campaign had changed.

"This campaign has always been about fighting for Oklahoma families, workers and businesses, and that mission continues with the same energy and determination as ever," Tedford said in a statement.

David responded to Trump's endorsement of Lahmeyer in a post on X, saying she respected the president but would keep working to earn the trust of voters.

"Outside voices don't decide Oklahoma elections," she said. "The people of Oklahoma do."

(This story was updated to add new information.)

This article originally appeared on Oklahoman: Donald Trump endorses Jackson Lahmeyer in key Oklahoma primary race

value.Title: Pakistan lacks clear industrialization roadmap and needs stable policies to attract Chinese green investments: Experts
value.Overview: Pakistan News Gazette, 726words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-J163-RRM4-Y3FG-00000-00Pakistan lacks clear industrialization roadmap and needs stable policies to attract Chinese green investments: Experts2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:00ZLexisNexisPakistan lacks clear industrialization roadmap and needs stable policies to attract Chinese green investments: Experts

Experts at a policy consultation said that Pakistan must undertake structural reforms, ensure policy consistency, and offer competitive incentives to attract Chinese private sector investment, particularly in emerging sectors such as renewable energy, electric vehicles (EVs), and green manufacturing.

The consultation on 'How Pakistan Can Compete for China's Green Manufacturing FDI', was organized by Sustainable Development Policy Institute (SDPI) here Monday.

Speaking on the occasion, Mustafa Hyder Sayed, Executive Director, Pakistan-China Institute, said that while China-Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC) had made significant progress through Chinese state-owned enterprises (SOEs), future growth depended on attracting Chinese private sector firms relocating abroad.

Rooftop solarization offers a viable entry point for such engagement, he said and suggested that Pakistan must compete with ASEAN economies like Vietnam and Malaysia, which provided better infrastructure, streamlined procedures and investor-friendly policies. He called for joint planning with Chinese investors, provision of subsidized land, tax holidays and duty exemptions on imported machinery, along with an effective one-window facilitation system.

Xiaokang Xue, Researcher at the Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University, highlighted a surge in Chinese overseas investments in clean technology manufacturing, particularly in ASEAN and Central Asia. He said host countries must ensure market access, raw material availability and long-term policy clarity to attract such investments. He added that Pakistan has potential in solar and EV sectors but must offer fiscal incentives comparable to countries like Indonesia, Thailand and Malaysia.

Prof. Stella Hong Zhang, Assistant Professor, International Studies, Hamilton Lugar School of Global and International Studies, Indiana University Bloomington, compared ASEAN and Middle East and North Africa (MENA) models, stating that ASEAN countries have successfully integrated into global manufacturing through strong industrial policies, SEZs, and export-oriented strategies.

Engr. Ubaid ur Rehman Zia, Head of Energy Unit at SDPI, highlighted that Pakistan's energy partnership with China was entering a new phase, moving beyond the traditional sovereign guarantee-based model established under the CPEC. While earlier engagement was driven by large-scale, state-led investments financed through sovereign-backed debt, there is now a visible shift towards more commercially oriented and diversified models, he said. Increasingly, he maintained, Chinese energy engagement is being shaped by equity participation, joint ventures, and private sector actors alongside growing demand for decentralized and cost-effective energy solutions in Pakistan's industrial sector. This evolving landscape, he said, underscores the need to better understand how investment structures, risk-sharing mechanisms, and emerging business models are transforming bilateral energy cooperation.

Dr Hassan Daud Butt, Senior Advisor, China Energy Engineering Group, said global investment patterns are shifting toward 'friend-shoring' and 'near-shoring,' where stability and trust outweigh cost advantages. He stressed that Pakistan must develop reliable, cost-efficient, and green energy ecosystems to remain competitive. He noted that future foreign investment would focus on energy-efficient technologies, smart grids and low-carbon manufacturing, driven by demand from advanced sectors such as AI data centers. Dr. Butt emphasized the need for a facilitating state to support industrial upgrading, innovation, and diversification, adding that targeted industrial policy, sector prioritization, and performance-based incentives are critical for sustainable growth.

Prof. M A Jinlong, School of Architecture, APEC Sustainable Energy Center, Tianjin University, highlighted that energy security was now central to investment decisions, particularly amid global geopolitical uncertainties. He stressed the need for strengthening Pakistan's power grid, ensure reliable electricity supply to special economic zones (SEZs), and promote low-carbon energy to meet international market standards. He also underscored the importance of efficient regulatory procedures, investor-friendly policies, and development of AI and circular economy infrastructure.

SECTION 1 RECENT NEWS

Fri Apr 24, 2026: Duke Energy Secures 20-Year License for Robinson Nuclear Plant

Duke Energy Corporation has received a significant boost with the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission's (NRC) decision to grant a 20-year extension for the operating license of its Robinson Nuclear Plant. This renewal allows the facility to continue producing energy until the year 2050, a crucial development for the utility giant. The announcement was made on April 23rd, 2026, and signifies Duke Energy's commitment to providing stable and sustainable energy to the region.

The Robinson Nuclear Plant, located in Hartsville, South Carolina, plays a vital role in Duke Energy's operations, supplying electricity to approximately 570,000 households. This license extension not only secures the plant's operational future but also aligns with Duke Energy's broader strategy of maintaining a diverse energy portfolio. The company is focused on integrating nuclear, natural gas, and renewable energy sources to meet the growing energy demands of its 8 million customers across various states, including the Carolinas, Indiana, Florida, Ohio, and Kentucky.

Duke Energy operates within the regulated utilities sector, boasting a market capitalization of approximately $99.64 billion. The company is segmented into electric utilities and infrastructure, gas utilities and infrastructure, and commercial renewables. Currently, the company's P/E ratio stands at 20.26, which is close to its five-year median, indicating that the stock may be fairly valued in the current market context. Investors are encouraged to consider this valuation alongside other financial metrics when evaluating the stock's potential.

The GF Score(TradeMark) for Duke Energy is 77 out of 100, reflecting strong performance across key metrics that can lead to higher long-term returns. The company's profitability rank is notably strong at 7 out of 10, underscoring its operational efficiency. However, concerns regarding financial strength persist, with a rating of just 3 out of 10, suggesting potential issues related to debt levels and overall financial health. Investors should carefully weigh these factors when considering Duke Energy as part of their investment portfolio.

In recent months, insider trading activity has raised eyebrows, with insiders selling $5.6 million worth of shares and no recorded insider buying. This trend may lead to questions about insider confidence in the company's future performance. Overall, Duke Energy presents a mixed outlook for investors. While the recent license renewal for the Robinson Nuclear Plant strengthens its operational foundation, its financial challenges could impact long-term growth. Investors are encouraged to explore further insights about Duke Energy and its current market position.

Recent News 2 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 271 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Honored with Arbor Day Foundation’s Tree Line USA Award for 20th Year

Duke Energy Corporation has received national recognition for its tree care practices for the 20th consecutive year. The Arbor Day Foundation named Duke Energy Florida a 'Tree Line USA' utility, citing the company's rigorous tree pruning standards, comprehensive employee training, and public education efforts aimed at reducing power outages and safeguarding electrical infrastructure.

On 22nd April 2026, the Arbor Day Foundation lauded Duke Energy for meeting industry standards for tree care, supporting tree planting initiatives, and engaging in annual arbor education programs. The designation reflects Duke Energy's commitment to sustainable practices, as well as its proactive approach to maintaining the health of urban trees in proximity to electrical lines.

One of Duke Energy's ongoing initiatives includes an annual free tree giveaway for customers through the Arbor Day Foundation's Energy-Saving Trees program. In 2026, the company distributed 1,000 trees to Florida customers, bringing the total number of free trees given away statewide to over 16,000 since 2017. These trees are shipped directly to customers with detailed planting instructions designed to encourage proper placement away from power lines.

Company officials noted that proper tree placement is essential for conserving energy and minimizing the risk of outages during storm and hurricane seasons. Duke Energy also conducts year-round vegetation maintenance near power lines and ramps up these efforts ahead of hurricane season, ensuring a reliable power supply for its customers.

Additionally, Duke Energy reminds customers not to trim trees near power lines themselves and encourages them to contact Sunshine 811 before digging near underground utilities. More information about responsible tree planting near electrical equipment can be found through Duke Energy's Right Tree Right Place program.

Recent News 3 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 395 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Foundation Launches America250 Grants for South Carolina Green Spaces

GREENVILLE, S.C.– To strengthen communities across South Carolina as the nation approaches its 250th anniversary, Duke Energy Foundation is awarding $275,000 to nonprofit organizations through a first round of funding as part of its America250 initiative. This funding, announced on 22nd April 2026, celebrates Earth Day and aims to enhance local parks, tree planting efforts, and trail maintenance projects throughout the Palmetto State.

The grants, which form part of a total investment exceeding $550,000 in South Carolina, are aimed at revitalizing parks and shared green spaces, supporting tree planting, and litter prevention initiatives. "As we look back on our country's 250-year anniversary, it's an opportunity to reflect on our nation's history while working to support the next generation," said Tim Pearson, Duke Energy South Carolina president. "We are proud to partner with local organizations who can help care for the Palmetto State now, so future generations can enjoy its beauty."

Duke Energy Foundation has awarded a total of 11 grants in this first round of funding. Notable recipients include the South Carolina Wildlife Federation, Foothills Trail Conservancy, and the Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance. Each organization is expected to utilize the funding to enhance local environments and foster community engagement in conservation efforts. For instance, Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance plans to create a new trail that highlights the scenic beauty of Lake Keowee while educating visitors about the Revolutionary War history in the area.

The positive responses from grant recipients highlight the importance of such initiatives. Matt Flege, Executive Director of Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance, expressed gratitude for the support, stating, "The America250 grant will allow us to create a new trail showcasing the beauty of Lake Keowee to South Carolina residents and visitors alike." Similarly, Susan H. Wild, Chair of Sumter Green, emphasized how the investment strengthens their commitment to beautifying the community, ensuring that efforts made today will benefit future generations.

These grants mark the first awards under Duke Energy Foundation's America250 initiative, which represents a commitment of over $1 million towards community-driven projects across the company's six-state service territories. Additional grant recipients will be announced later this spring, including initiatives focused on veterans' workforce pathways and enhancing access to history and civics education. Duke Energy Foundation continues to provide nearly $30 million annually in philanthropic support to the communities it serves, reflecting its dedication to environmental stewardship and community development.

SECTION 2 TODAY'S BULLISH SIGNALS

2.1 Uptrend

Today's Volatility:

The 5% discount to 12-month high of $134.49 against the 19% premium to 12-month low of $107.42 suggests the stock continues to push for new highs.

Price/Moving Average Price of 1.04:

- The Price/MAP 200 for Duke Energy Corporation is 1.04. Being higher than 1 is a bullish indicator. The stock is trading above both its MAPs and the 50-day MAP of $133.05 is higher than the 200-day MAP of $122.54, a second bullish indicator. The 200-day MAP has increased to $122.54, a third bullish indicator.

Past Quarter:

- In the last three months the stock has hit a new 52-week high twelve times, pointing to a significant uptrend.

2.2 Other Bullish Signals

- Return on Equity of 9.0% versus sector average of 5.8%.

MCap/Total Assets:

- Tobin's Q Ratio, defined as MCap divided by Total Assets, is 0.5. Compared with the rest of the market the stock is undervalued.

- Net profit margin has averaged 14.4% in the last 3 years. This is better than the sector average of 11.8% and suggests a high margin of safety.

- As per the Du Pont analysis, Return on Equity of 9.0% is better than sector average of 5.8%. This is computed as net profit margin of 15.2% times asset turnover [sales/assets] of 0.16 times leverage factor [total assets/shareholders' equity] of 3.6.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

Source: New York Stock Exchange: https://www.nasdaq.com/market-activity/stocks/DUK, company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

SECTION 3 ONGOING BULLISH PARAMETERS

3.1 Rule of 40

The stock scores a favorable score exceeding 40 when using the Rule of 40 (Revenue Growth plus EBITDA margin). Y.o.y revenue growth of 4%, EBITDA margin is 36.2% and the sum of the two 40.7% needs to exceed 40%.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

3.2 Created Market Value [CMV] past 17 yrs of $78.6 billion

- Market Capitalization has increased by $80.7 billion from $18.4 billion to $99.1 billion in the last 17 years. This increase comprises cumulative retained earnings (RETE) of $2 billion and Created Market Value of $78.6 billion. The Created Market Value multiple, defined by the change in MCap for every $1 of retained earnings is exemplary at $39.68.

3.3 Satisfies four criteria of Benjamin Graham

- The P/E of 22.3 is 0.4 times the highest average P/E of 57.7 in the last five years. This is a value criterion, according to Benjamin Graham who described as a value criterion "A P/E ratio down to less than four-tenth of the highest average P/E ratio the stock attained in the most recent five years".

-""Earnings growth over the most recent ten years of 7% compounded - that is a doubling of earnings in a five-year period""; the earnings per share have more than doubled in the last 5 years.

-""Stability of growth in earnings over the last 5 years, defined as no more than two declines of 5% or greater in year-end earnings""; there have been 2 declines in earnings in the last 5 years.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

3.4 Satisfies 5 out of 9 criteria of Joseph Piotroski [pass mark 5]:

- Positive net income.

- Positive operating cashflow.

- Return on Assets improvement [from 2.4% to 2.5%].

- Good quality of earnings [operating cashflow exceeds net income].

- Improvement in gross margin [from 98.0% to 98.1%].

But does not meet the following 4 criteria of Joseph Piotroski:

- Improvement in long-term debt to total assets.

- Improvement in current ratio.

- Total shares on issue unchanged (or reduction in total shares on issue).

- Improvement in asset turnover.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

SECTION 5 PEER COMPARISON & RANKING OF DUK

5.1 LONG-TERM FUNDAMENTAL RANKING: 3 OUT OF 5 [5 is best]

Duke Energy Corporation is ranked number 1 out of 32 listed utility companies in the United States with a market capitalization of $99.1 billion.

Within its sector it has a relatively low P/E of 22.3 and low P/E/G of 0.36.

It has a moderate relative ROE of 9.0% and ROA of 2.5%. The company paid a dividend of $3.19 in the last twelve months. The dividend yield is moderate at 3.3%. Finally, its earnings growth in the past 12 months has been a comparatively high 61.3%.

Stocks are scored on a set of parameters reflecting fundamental analytical tools involving valuation, size and financial performance. They are ranked according to the average values of those parameters. The highest ranking is 5 and the lowest ranking is 1.

value.Title: Pakistan lacks a clear industrialization roadmap and needs stable policies to attract Chinese green investments: Experts
value.Overview: The Frontier Star, Vol. 31; No. 123, 769words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-PFM3-RRTC-W2YC-00000-00Pakistan lacks a clear industrialization roadmap and needs stable policies to attract Chinese green investments: Experts2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:00ZLexisNexisPakistan lacks a clear industrialization roadmap and needs stable policies to attract Chinese green investments: Experts

Experts at a policy consultation said that Pakistan must undertake structural reforms, ensure policy consistency, and offer competitive incentives to attract Chinese private sector investment, particularly in emerging sectors such as renewable energy, electric vehicles (EVs), and green manufacturing.

The consultation on 'How Pakistan Can Compete for China's Green Manufacturing FDI', was organized by Sustainable Development Policy Institute (SDPI) here on Monday.

Mustafa Hyder Sayed, Executive Director, Pakistan-China Institute, said that while China-Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC) has made significant progress through Chinese state-owned enterprises (SOEs), future growth depends on attracting Chinese private sector firms relocating abroad.

Rooftop solarization offers a viable entry point for such engagement, he said and suggested that Pakistan must compete with ASEAN economies like Vietnam and Malaysia, which provide better infrastructure, streamlined procedures, and investor-friendly policies. He called for joint planning with Chinese investors, provision of subsidized land, tax holidays, and duty exemptions on imported machinery, along with an effective one-window facilitation system.

Xiaokang Xue, Researcher at the Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University, highlighted a surge in Chinese overseas investments in clean technology manufacturing, particularly in ASEAN and Central Asia. He said host countries must ensure market access, raw material availability, and long-term policy clarity to attract such investments. He added that Pakistan has potential in solar and EV sectors but must offer fiscal incentives comparable to countries like Indonesia, Thailand, and Malaysia.

Prof. Stella Hong Zhang, Assistant Professor, International Studies, Hamilton Lugar School of Global and International Studies, Indiana University Bloomington, compared ASEAN and Middle East and North Africa (MENA) models, stating that ASEAN countries have successfully integrated into global manufacturing through strong industrial policies, SEZs, and export-oriented strategies. She noted that Indonesia has leveraged its nickel reserves to attract Chinese investment in EV and battery manufacturing, while Pakistan lacks a clear industrialization roadmap and continues to rely on imports of Chinese solar technologies instead of developing domestic capacity.

Engineer Ubaid ur Rehman Zia, Head of Energy Unit at SDPI, highlighted that Pakistan's energy partnership with China is entering a new phase, moving beyond the traditional sovereign guarantee-based model established under the CPEC. While earlier engagement was driven by large-scale, state-led investments financed through sovereign-backed debt, there is now a visible shift towards more commercially oriented and diversified models, he said. Increasingly, he maintained, Chinese energy engagement is being shaped by equity participation, joint ventures, and private sector actors alongside growing demand for decentralized and cost-effective energy solutions in Pakistan's industrial sector. This evolving landscape, he said, underscores the need to better understand how investment structures, risk-sharing mechanisms, and emerging business models are transforming bilateral energy cooperation.

Dr. Hassan Daud Butt, Senior Advisor, China Energy Engineering Group, said global investment patterns are shifting toward 'friend-shoring' and 'near-shoring,' where stability and trust outweigh cost advantages. He stressed that Pakistan must develop reliable, cost-efficient, and green energy ecosystems to remain competitive.

He noted that future foreign investment will focus on energy-efficient technologies, smart grids, and low-carbon manufacturing, driven by demand from advanced sectors such as AI data centers. Dr. Butt emphasized the need for a facilitating state to support industrial upgrading, innovation, and diversification, adding that targeted industrial policy, sector prioritization, and performance-based incentives are critical for sustainable growth.

Prof. MA Jinlong, School of Architecture, APEC Sustainable Energy Center, Tianjin University, highlighted that energy security is now central to investment decisions, particularly amid global geopolitical uncertainties. He stressed the need for strengthening Pakistan's power grid, ensure reliable electricity supply to special economic zones (SEZs), and promote low-carbon energy to meet international market standards. He also underscored the importance of efficient regulatory procedures, investor-friendly policies, and development of AI and circular economy infrastructure.

Fri Apr 24, 2026: Duke Energy Secures 20-Year License for Robinson Nuclear Plant

Duke Energy Corporation has received a significant boost with the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission's (NRC) decision to grant a 20-year extension for the operating license of its Robinson Nuclear Plant. This renewal allows the facility to continue producing energy until the year 2050, a crucial development for the utility giant. The announcement was made on April 23rd, 2026, and signifies Duke Energy's commitment to providing stable and sustainable energy to the region.

The Robinson Nuclear Plant, located in Hartsville, South Carolina, plays a vital role in Duke Energy's operations, supplying electricity to approximately 570,000 households. This license extension not only secures the plant's operational future but also aligns with Duke Energy's broader strategy of maintaining a diverse energy portfolio. The company is focused on integrating nuclear, natural gas, and renewable energy sources to meet the growing energy demands of its 8 million customers across various states, including the Carolinas, Indiana, Florida, Ohio, and Kentucky.

Duke Energy operates within the regulated utilities sector, boasting a market capitalization of approximately $99.64 billion. The company is segmented into electric utilities and infrastructure, gas utilities and infrastructure, and commercial renewables. Currently, the company's P/E ratio stands at 20.26, which is close to its five-year median, indicating that the stock may be fairly valued in the current market context. Investors are encouraged to consider this valuation alongside other financial metrics when evaluating the stock's potential.

The GF Score(TradeMark) for Duke Energy is 77 out of 100, reflecting strong performance across key metrics that can lead to higher long-term returns. The company's profitability rank is notably strong at 7 out of 10, underscoring its operational efficiency. However, concerns regarding financial strength persist, with a rating of just 3 out of 10, suggesting potential issues related to debt levels and overall financial health. Investors should carefully weigh these factors when considering Duke Energy as part of their investment portfolio.

In recent months, insider trading activity has raised eyebrows, with insiders selling $5.6 million worth of shares and no recorded insider buying. This trend may lead to questions about insider confidence in the company's future performance. Overall, Duke Energy presents a mixed outlook for investors. While the recent license renewal for the Robinson Nuclear Plant strengthens its operational foundation, its financial challenges could impact long-term growth. Investors are encouraged to explore further insights about Duke Energy and its current market position.

Recent News 2 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 395 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Foundation Launches America250 Grants for South Carolina Green Spaces

GREENVILLE, S.C.– To strengthen communities across South Carolina as the nation approaches its 250th anniversary, Duke Energy Foundation is awarding $275,000 to nonprofit organizations through a first round of funding as part of its America250 initiative. This funding, announced on 22nd April 2026, celebrates Earth Day and aims to enhance local parks, tree planting efforts, and trail maintenance projects throughout the Palmetto State.

The grants, which form part of a total investment exceeding $550,000 in South Carolina, are aimed at revitalizing parks and shared green spaces, supporting tree planting, and litter prevention initiatives. "As we look back on our country's 250-year anniversary, it's an opportunity to reflect on our nation's history while working to support the next generation," said Tim Pearson, Duke Energy South Carolina president. "We are proud to partner with local organizations who can help care for the Palmetto State now, so future generations can enjoy its beauty."

Duke Energy Foundation has awarded a total of 11 grants in this first round of funding. Notable recipients include the South Carolina Wildlife Federation, Foothills Trail Conservancy, and the Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance. Each organization is expected to utilize the funding to enhance local environments and foster community engagement in conservation efforts. For instance, Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance plans to create a new trail that highlights the scenic beauty of Lake Keowee while educating visitors about the Revolutionary War history in the area.

The positive responses from grant recipients highlight the importance of such initiatives. Matt Flege, Executive Director of Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance, expressed gratitude for the support, stating, "The America250 grant will allow us to create a new trail showcasing the beauty of Lake Keowee to South Carolina residents and visitors alike." Similarly, Susan H. Wild, Chair of Sumter Green, emphasized how the investment strengthens their commitment to beautifying the community, ensuring that efforts made today will benefit future generations.

These grants mark the first awards under Duke Energy Foundation's America250 initiative, which represents a commitment of over $1 million towards community-driven projects across the company's six-state service territories. Additional grant recipients will be announced later this spring, including initiatives focused on veterans' workforce pathways and enhancing access to history and civics education. Duke Energy Foundation continues to provide nearly $30 million annually in philanthropic support to the communities it serves, reflecting its dedication to environmental stewardship and community development.

Recent News 3 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 271 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Honored with Arbor Day Foundation’s Tree Line USA Award for 20th Year

Duke Energy Corporation has received national recognition for its tree care practices for the 20th consecutive year. The Arbor Day Foundation named Duke Energy Florida a 'Tree Line USA' utility, citing the company's rigorous tree pruning standards, comprehensive employee training, and public education efforts aimed at reducing power outages and safeguarding electrical infrastructure.

On 22nd April 2026, the Arbor Day Foundation lauded Duke Energy for meeting industry standards for tree care, supporting tree planting initiatives, and engaging in annual arbor education programs. The designation reflects Duke Energy's commitment to sustainable practices, as well as its proactive approach to maintaining the health of urban trees in proximity to electrical lines.

One of Duke Energy's ongoing initiatives includes an annual free tree giveaway for customers through the Arbor Day Foundation's Energy-Saving Trees program. In 2026, the company distributed 1,000 trees to Florida customers, bringing the total number of free trees given away statewide to over 16,000 since 2017. These trees are shipped directly to customers with detailed planting instructions designed to encourage proper placement away from power lines.

Company officials noted that proper tree placement is essential for conserving energy and minimizing the risk of outages during storm and hurricane seasons. Duke Energy also conducts year-round vegetation maintenance near power lines and ramps up these efforts ahead of hurricane season, ensuring a reliable power supply for its customers.

Additionally, Duke Energy reminds customers not to trim trees near power lines themselves and encourages them to contact Sunshine 811 before digging near underground utilities. More information about responsible tree planting near electrical equipment can be found through Duke Energy's Right Tree Right Place program.

The stock is trading above both its MAPs and the 50-day MAP of $133.05 is higher than the 200-day MAP of $122.54, a second bullish indicator. The 200-day MAP has increased to $122.54, a third bullish indicator.

MCap/Total Assets:

- Net profit margin has averaged 14.4% in the last 3 years. This is better than the sector average of 11.8% and suggests a high margin of safety.

3.1 Rule of 40

The stock scores a favorable score exceeding 40 when using the Rule of 40 (Revenue Growth plus EBITDA margin). Y.o.y revenue growth of 4%, EBITDA margin is 36.2% and the sum of the two 40.7% needs to exceed 40%.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

3.2 Created Market Value [CMV] past 17 yrs of $78.6 billion

- Market Capitalization has increased by $80.7 billion from $18.4 billion to $99.1 billion in the last 17 years. This increase comprises cumulative retained earnings (RETE) of $2 billion and Created Market Value of $78.6 billion. The Created Market Value multiple, defined by the change in MCap for every $1 of retained earnings is exemplary at $39.68.

3.3 Satisfies four criteria of Benjamin Graham

- The P/E of 22.3 is 0.4 times the highest average P/E of 57.7 in the last five years. This is a value criterion, according to Benjamin Graham who described as a value criterion "A P/E ratio down to less than four-tenth of the highest average P/E ratio the stock attained in the most recent five years".

-""Earnings growth over the most recent ten years of 7% compounded - that is a doubling of earnings in a five-year period""; the earnings per share have more than doubled in the last 5 years.

-""Stability of growth in earnings over the last 5 years, defined as no more than two declines of 5% or greater in year-end earnings""; there have been 2 declines in earnings in the last 5 years.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

3.4 Satisfies 5 out of 9 criteria of Joseph Piotroski [pass mark 5]:

- Positive net income.

- Positive operating cashflow.

- Return on Assets improvement [from 2.4% to 2.5%].

- Good quality of earnings [operating cashflow exceeds net income].

- Improvement in gross margin [from 98.0% to 98.1%].

But does not meet the following 4 criteria of Joseph Piotroski:

- Improvement in long-term debt to total assets.

- Improvement in current ratio.

- Total shares on issue unchanged (or reduction in total shares on issue).

- Improvement in asset turnover.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

SECTION 4 TODAY'S BEARISH SIGNALS

4.1 Other Bearish Signals

- Total Liabilities/EBITDA of 12.3 is more than 5, this compares unfavourably with the Joseph Piotroski benchmark of 5.

5.1 LONG-TERM FUNDAMENTAL RANKING: 3 OUT OF 5 [5 is best]

Duke Energy Corporation is ranked number 1 out of 32 listed utility companies in the United States with a market capitalization of $99.1 billion.

Within its sector it has a relatively low P/E of 22.3 and low P/E/G of 0.36.

It has a moderate relative ROE of 9.0% and ROA of 2.5%. The company paid a dividend of $3.19 in the last twelve months. The dividend yield is moderate at 3.3%. Finally, its earnings growth in the past 12 months has been a comparatively high 61.3%.

value.Title: Pakistan lacks clear industrialization roadmap and needs stable policies to attract Chinese green investments: Experts
value.Overview: The Balochistan Times (AsiaNet), Vol. 49; No. 123, 726words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-PFM3-RRTC-W2PS-00000-00Pakistan lacks clear industrialization roadmap and needs stable policies to attract Chinese green investments: Experts2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:00ZLexisNexisPakistan lacks clear industrialization roadmap and needs stable policies to attract Chinese green investments: Experts

Experts at a policy consultation said that Pakistan must undertake structural reforms, ensure policy consistency, and offer competitive incentives to attract Chinese private sector investment, particularly in emerging sectors such as renewable energy, electric vehicles (EVs), and green manufacturing.

The consultation on 'How Pakistan Can Compete for China's Green Manufacturing FDI', was organized by Sustainable Development Policy Institute (SDPI) here Monday.

Speaking on the occasion, Mustafa Hyder Sayed, Executive Director, Pakistan-China Institute, said that while China-Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC) had made significant progress through Chinese state-owned enterprises (SOEs), future growth depended on attracting Chinese private sector firms relocating abroad.

Rooftop solarization offers a viable entry point for such engagement, he said and suggested that Pakistan must compete with ASEAN economies like Vietnam and Malaysia, which provided better infrastructure, streamlined procedures and investor-friendly policies. He called for joint planning with Chinese investors, provision of subsidized land, tax holidays and duty exemptions on imported machinery, along with an effective one-window facilitation system.

Xiaokang Xue, Researcher at the Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University, highlighted a surge in Chinese overseas investments in clean technology manufacturing, particularly in ASEAN and Central Asia. He said host countries must ensure market access, raw material availability and long-term policy clarity to attract such investments. He added that Pakistan has potential in solar and EV sectors but must offer fiscal incentives comparable to countries like Indonesia, Thailand and Malaysia.

Prof. Stella Hong Zhang, Assistant Professor, International Studies, Hamilton Lugar School of Global and International Studies, Indiana University Bloomington, compared ASEAN and Middle East and North Africa (MENA) models, stating that ASEAN countries have successfully integrated into global manufacturing through strong industrial policies, SEZs, and export-oriented strategies.

Engr. Ubaid ur Rehman Zia, Head of Energy Unit at SDPI, highlighted that Pakistan's energy partnership with China was entering a new phase, moving beyond the traditional sovereign guarantee-based model established under the CPEC. While earlier engagement was driven by large-scale, state-led investments financed through sovereign-backed debt, there is now a visible shift towards more commercially oriented and diversified models, he said. Increasingly, he maintained, Chinese energy engagement is being shaped by equity participation, joint ventures, and private sector actors alongside growing demand for decentralized and cost-effective energy solutions in Pakistan's industrial sector. This evolving landscape, he said, underscores the need to better understand how investment structures, risk-sharing mechanisms, and emerging business models are transforming bilateral energy cooperation.

Dr Hassan Daud Butt, Senior Advisor, China Energy Engineering Group, said global investment patterns are shifting toward 'friend-shoring' and 'near-shoring,' where stability and trust outweigh cost advantages. He stressed that Pakistan must develop reliable, cost-efficient, and green energy ecosystems to remain competitive. He noted that future foreign investment would focus on energy-efficient technologies, smart grids and low-carbon manufacturing, driven by demand from advanced sectors such as AI data centers. Dr. Butt emphasized the need for a facilitating state to support industrial upgrading, innovation, and diversification, adding that targeted industrial policy, sector prioritization, and performance-based incentives are critical for sustainable growth.

Prof. M A Jinlong, School of Architecture, APEC Sustainable Energy Center, Tianjin University, highlighted that energy security was now central to investment decisions, particularly amid global geopolitical uncertainties. He stressed the need for strengthening Pakistan's power grid, ensure reliable electricity supply to special economic zones (SEZs), and promote low-carbon energy to meet international market standards. He also underscored the importance of efficient regulatory procedures, investor-friendly policies, and development of AI and circular economy infrastructure.

urn:contentItem:6JM6-89F3-RVVW-V05C-00000-00NJBPU launches sweeping utility reform review2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:48:49ZLexisNexisNJBPU launches sweeping utility reform reviewMatthew FazelpoorNew BrunswickFULL TEXT

The basics:NJBPU launched a major review of New Jersey utility regulationStudy will examine performance-based ratemaking and multiyear rate plansOfficials aim to improve affordability, reliability, utility accountabilityReview could reshape how electric utilities earn revenue, set ratesThe New Jersey Board of Public Utilities formally launched what officials described as one of the most significant reviews of utility regulation in decades May 7. The broad stakeholder process could ultimately reshape how New Jersey’s electric utilities earn revenue and how customer rates are set.The session took place at Mercer County Community College in West Windsor. It marked the official kickoff of the state’s Utility Business Model Reform Study. Gov. Mikie Sherrill ordered thesweeping examination under Executive Order No. 1, which she signed during her inauguration.The study comes as New Jersey residents and businesses continue to grapple with rising electricity costs driven by a combination of energy market pressures, infrastructure investments and growing demand on the electric grid.“This proceeding is one of the most consequential regulatory reviews New Jersey has taken on in a generation,” said NJBPU President Christine Guhl-Sadovy. “It goes to the core question of how utilities should earn revenue in a modern energy system.It goes to the core question of how utilities should earn revenue in a modern energy system.Christine Guhl-Sadovy, president, NJBPU"As we examine new approaches, our priority is ensuring we balance reliability with affordability, and tie utility profits with performance for the benefit of New Jersey ratepayers.”Seeking stabilityAt the center of the review is whether New Jersey should move away from the traditional utility model, which largely rewards utilities for infrastructure spending. It could instead adopt a more performance-based regulation, tying earnings closer to customer outcomes such as reliability, affordability, customer service and renewable energy integration.The board’s consultant-led study will evaluate options including performance-based ratemaking, multiyear rate plans, alternative cost recovery structures and others. The reforms are designed to stabilize customer bills and reduce the cycle of repeated rate increases.Last week's meeting brought together former utility commissioners from Hawaii, Indiana, Massachusetts and Illinois. All have overseen performance-based regulatory reforms in their states. Officials said the goal is to gather lessons from states that have already experimented with newer utility regulatory frameworks.This is the momentRepresentatives from utilities and other stakeholders signaled cautious support for examining reforms.Joe Accardo is senior vice president of state regulatory affairs and centralized services for Public Service Enterprise Group. He said New Jersey’s current regulatory framework has historically helped support investments in energy efficiency, solar deployment and infrastructure modernization. However, he argued the state must adapt to mounting affordability and energy supply pressures.“Now is the time to examine a more modern approach to setting rates that achieves more stable, predictable rates for customers and that factors utility performance into how much our customers should have to pay,” said Accardo. He added that “the good news is that New Jersey need not fully reinvent the wheel in how it develops just and reasonable rates.” Instead, it should modernize rate frameworks using approaches already proving successful elsewhere, he said.Accardo also pointed to the value of more forward-looking planning and performance incentives.As already recognized by many of our peer states, utility rates can be made more stable and transparent through up-front planning and rate review.Joe Accardo, senior vice president of state regulatory affairs and centralized services, PSEG“As already recognized by many of our peer states, utility rates can be made more stable and transparent through up-front planning and rate review,” he said. “More forward-looking rate planning allows visibility for big expenses and spreads the costs more evenly over time and equitably through rate design.”The proceeding will now move into a longer-term study phase led by the board’s independent consultant. NJBPU expects to hold additional stakeholder sessions and public comment periods in the coming months. Written comments on the proceeding are due by returned by May 29.While the review will not result in immediate rate changes, officials said its recommendations could shape the most significant restructuring of New Jersey utility regulation in decades.Watch the full NJBPU session:https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=q-WlZxuXIM0&t=563s

Fri Apr 24, 2026: Duke Energy Secures 20-Year License for Robinson Nuclear Plant

Duke Energy Corporation has received a significant boost with the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission's (NRC) decision to grant a 20-year extension for the operating license of its Robinson Nuclear Plant. This renewal allows the facility to continue producing energy until the year 2050, a crucial development for the utility giant. The announcement was made on April 23rd, 2026, and signifies Duke Energy's commitment to providing stable and sustainable energy to the region.

The Robinson Nuclear Plant, located in Hartsville, South Carolina, plays a vital role in Duke Energy's operations, supplying electricity to approximately 570,000 households. This license extension not only secures the plant's operational future but also aligns with Duke Energy's broader strategy of maintaining a diverse energy portfolio. The company is focused on integrating nuclear, natural gas, and renewable energy sources to meet the growing energy demands of its 8 million customers across various states, including the Carolinas, Indiana, Florida, Ohio, and Kentucky.

Duke Energy operates within the regulated utilities sector, boasting a market capitalization of approximately $99.64 billion. The company is segmented into electric utilities and infrastructure, gas utilities and infrastructure, and commercial renewables. Currently, the company's P/E ratio stands at 20.26, which is close to its five-year median, indicating that the stock may be fairly valued in the current market context. Investors are encouraged to consider this valuation alongside other financial metrics when evaluating the stock's potential.

The GF Score(TradeMark) for Duke Energy is 77 out of 100, reflecting strong performance across key metrics that can lead to higher long-term returns. The company's profitability rank is notably strong at 7 out of 10, underscoring its operational efficiency. However, concerns regarding financial strength persist, with a rating of just 3 out of 10, suggesting potential issues related to debt levels and overall financial health. Investors should carefully weigh these factors when considering Duke Energy as part of their investment portfolio.

In recent months, insider trading activity has raised eyebrows, with insiders selling $5.6 million worth of shares and no recorded insider buying. This trend may lead to questions about insider confidence in the company's future performance. Overall, Duke Energy presents a mixed outlook for investors. While the recent license renewal for the Robinson Nuclear Plant strengthens its operational foundation, its financial challenges could impact long-term growth. Investors are encouraged to explore further insights about Duke Energy and its current market position.

Recent News 2 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 271 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Honored with Arbor Day Foundation’s Tree Line USA Award for 20th Year

Duke Energy Corporation has received national recognition for its tree care practices for the 20th consecutive year. The Arbor Day Foundation named Duke Energy Florida a 'Tree Line USA' utility, citing the company's rigorous tree pruning standards, comprehensive employee training, and public education efforts aimed at reducing power outages and safeguarding electrical infrastructure.

On 22nd April 2026, the Arbor Day Foundation lauded Duke Energy for meeting industry standards for tree care, supporting tree planting initiatives, and engaging in annual arbor education programs. The designation reflects Duke Energy's commitment to sustainable practices, as well as its proactive approach to maintaining the health of urban trees in proximity to electrical lines.

One of Duke Energy's ongoing initiatives includes an annual free tree giveaway for customers through the Arbor Day Foundation's Energy-Saving Trees program. In 2026, the company distributed 1,000 trees to Florida customers, bringing the total number of free trees given away statewide to over 16,000 since 2017. These trees are shipped directly to customers with detailed planting instructions designed to encourage proper placement away from power lines.

Company officials noted that proper tree placement is essential for conserving energy and minimizing the risk of outages during storm and hurricane seasons. Duke Energy also conducts year-round vegetation maintenance near power lines and ramps up these efforts ahead of hurricane season, ensuring a reliable power supply for its customers.

Additionally, Duke Energy reminds customers not to trim trees near power lines themselves and encourages them to contact Sunshine 811 before digging near underground utilities. More information about responsible tree planting near electrical equipment can be found through Duke Energy's Right Tree Right Place program.

Recent News 3 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 395 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Foundation Launches America250 Grants for South Carolina Green Spaces

GREENVILLE, S.C.– To strengthen communities across South Carolina as the nation approaches its 250th anniversary, Duke Energy Foundation is awarding $275,000 to nonprofit organizations through a first round of funding as part of its America250 initiative. This funding, announced on 22nd April 2026, celebrates Earth Day and aims to enhance local parks, tree planting efforts, and trail maintenance projects throughout the Palmetto State.

The grants, which form part of a total investment exceeding $550,000 in South Carolina, are aimed at revitalizing parks and shared green spaces, supporting tree planting, and litter prevention initiatives. "As we look back on our country's 250-year anniversary, it's an opportunity to reflect on our nation's history while working to support the next generation," said Tim Pearson, Duke Energy South Carolina president. "We are proud to partner with local organizations who can help care for the Palmetto State now, so future generations can enjoy its beauty."

Duke Energy Foundation has awarded a total of 11 grants in this first round of funding. Notable recipients include the South Carolina Wildlife Federation, Foothills Trail Conservancy, and the Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance. Each organization is expected to utilize the funding to enhance local environments and foster community engagement in conservation efforts. For instance, Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance plans to create a new trail that highlights the scenic beauty of Lake Keowee while educating visitors about the Revolutionary War history in the area.

The positive responses from grant recipients highlight the importance of such initiatives. Matt Flege, Executive Director of Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance, expressed gratitude for the support, stating, "The America250 grant will allow us to create a new trail showcasing the beauty of Lake Keowee to South Carolina residents and visitors alike." Similarly, Susan H. Wild, Chair of Sumter Green, emphasized how the investment strengthens their commitment to beautifying the community, ensuring that efforts made today will benefit future generations.

These grants mark the first awards under Duke Energy Foundation's America250 initiative, which represents a commitment of over $1 million towards community-driven projects across the company's six-state service territories. Additional grant recipients will be announced later this spring, including initiatives focused on veterans' workforce pathways and enhancing access to history and civics education. Duke Energy Foundation continues to provide nearly $30 million annually in philanthropic support to the communities it serves, reflecting its dedication to environmental stewardship and community development.

This is a value criterion, according to Benjamin Graham who described as a value criterion "A P/E ratio down to less than four-tenth of the highest average P/E ratio the stock attained in the most recent five years".

Fri Apr 24, 2026: Duke Energy Secures 20-Year License for Robinson Nuclear Plant

Duke Energy Corporation has received a significant boost with the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission's (NRC) decision to grant a 20-year extension for the operating license of its Robinson Nuclear Plant. This renewal allows the facility to continue producing energy until the year 2050, a crucial development for the utility giant. The announcement was made on April 23rd, 2026, and signifies Duke Energy's commitment to providing stable and sustainable energy to the region.

The Robinson Nuclear Plant, located in Hartsville, South Carolina, plays a vital role in Duke Energy's operations, supplying electricity to approximately 570,000 households. This license extension not only secures the plant's operational future but also aligns with Duke Energy's broader strategy of maintaining a diverse energy portfolio. The company is focused on integrating nuclear, natural gas, and renewable energy sources to meet the growing energy demands of its 8 million customers across various states, including the Carolinas, Indiana, Florida, Ohio, and Kentucky.

Duke Energy operates within the regulated utilities sector, boasting a market capitalization of approximately $99.64 billion. The company is segmented into electric utilities and infrastructure, gas utilities and infrastructure, and commercial renewables. Currently, the company's P/E ratio stands at 20.26, which is close to its five-year median, indicating that the stock may be fairly valued in the current market context. Investors are encouraged to consider this valuation alongside other financial metrics when evaluating the stock's potential.

The GF Score(TradeMark) for Duke Energy is 77 out of 100, reflecting strong performance across key metrics that can lead to higher long-term returns. The company's profitability rank is notably strong at 7 out of 10, underscoring its operational efficiency. However, concerns regarding financial strength persist, with a rating of just 3 out of 10, suggesting potential issues related to debt levels and overall financial health. Investors should carefully weigh these factors when considering Duke Energy as part of their investment portfolio.

In recent months, insider trading activity has raised eyebrows, with insiders selling $5.6 million worth of shares and no recorded insider buying. This trend may lead to questions about insider confidence in the company's future performance. Overall, Duke Energy presents a mixed outlook for investors. While the recent license renewal for the Robinson Nuclear Plant strengthens its operational foundation, its financial challenges could impact long-term growth. Investors are encouraged to explore further insights about Duke Energy and its current market position.

Recent News 2 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 271 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Honored with Arbor Day Foundation’s Tree Line USA Award for 20th Year

Duke Energy Corporation has received national recognition for its tree care practices for the 20th consecutive year. The Arbor Day Foundation named Duke Energy Florida a 'Tree Line USA' utility, citing the company's rigorous tree pruning standards, comprehensive employee training, and public education efforts aimed at reducing power outages and safeguarding electrical infrastructure.

On 22nd April 2026, the Arbor Day Foundation lauded Duke Energy for meeting industry standards for tree care, supporting tree planting initiatives, and engaging in annual arbor education programs. The designation reflects Duke Energy's commitment to sustainable practices, as well as its proactive approach to maintaining the health of urban trees in proximity to electrical lines.

One of Duke Energy's ongoing initiatives includes an annual free tree giveaway for customers through the Arbor Day Foundation's Energy-Saving Trees program. In 2026, the company distributed 1,000 trees to Florida customers, bringing the total number of free trees given away statewide to over 16,000 since 2017. These trees are shipped directly to customers with detailed planting instructions designed to encourage proper placement away from power lines.

Company officials noted that proper tree placement is essential for conserving energy and minimizing the risk of outages during storm and hurricane seasons. Duke Energy also conducts year-round vegetation maintenance near power lines and ramps up these efforts ahead of hurricane season, ensuring a reliable power supply for its customers.

Additionally, Duke Energy reminds customers not to trim trees near power lines themselves and encourages them to contact Sunshine 811 before digging near underground utilities. More information about responsible tree planting near electrical equipment can be found through Duke Energy's Right Tree Right Place program.

Recent News 3 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 395 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Foundation Launches America250 Grants for South Carolina Green Spaces

GREENVILLE, S.C.– To strengthen communities across South Carolina as the nation approaches its 250th anniversary, Duke Energy Foundation is awarding $275,000 to nonprofit organizations through a first round of funding as part of its America250 initiative. This funding, announced on 22nd April 2026, celebrates Earth Day and aims to enhance local parks, tree planting efforts, and trail maintenance projects throughout the Palmetto State.

The grants, which form part of a total investment exceeding $550,000 in South Carolina, are aimed at revitalizing parks and shared green spaces, supporting tree planting, and litter prevention initiatives. "As we look back on our country's 250-year anniversary, it's an opportunity to reflect on our nation's history while working to support the next generation," said Tim Pearson, Duke Energy South Carolina president. "We are proud to partner with local organizations who can help care for the Palmetto State now, so future generations can enjoy its beauty."

Duke Energy Foundation has awarded a total of 11 grants in this first round of funding. Notable recipients include the South Carolina Wildlife Federation, Foothills Trail Conservancy, and the Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance. Each organization is expected to utilize the funding to enhance local environments and foster community engagement in conservation efforts. For instance, Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance plans to create a new trail that highlights the scenic beauty of Lake Keowee while educating visitors about the Revolutionary War history in the area.

The positive responses from grant recipients highlight the importance of such initiatives. Matt Flege, Executive Director of Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance, expressed gratitude for the support, stating, "The America250 grant will allow us to create a new trail showcasing the beauty of Lake Keowee to South Carolina residents and visitors alike." Similarly, Susan H. Wild, Chair of Sumter Green, emphasized how the investment strengthens their commitment to beautifying the community, ensuring that efforts made today will benefit future generations.

These grants mark the first awards under Duke Energy Foundation's America250 initiative, which represents a commitment of over $1 million towards community-driven projects across the company's six-state service territories. Additional grant recipients will be announced later this spring, including initiatives focused on veterans' workforce pathways and enhancing access to history and civics education. Duke Energy Foundation continues to provide nearly $30 million annually in philanthropic support to the communities it serves, reflecting its dedication to environmental stewardship and community development.

SECTION 2 TODAY'S BULLISH SIGNALS

2.1 Uptrend

Today's Volatility:

The 4% discount to 12-month high of $134.49 against the 20% premium to 12-month low of $107.42 suggests the stock continues to push for new highs.

Today its volatility (highest price minus lowest price/lowest price) of 1.6% equalled the average daily volatility, up from 1.2584091808468567% on Wednesday. Price/Moving Average Price of 1.05:

- The Price/MAP 200 for Duke Energy Corporation is 1.05. Being higher than 1 is a bullish indicator. The stock is trading above both its MAPs and the 50-day MAP of $133.03 is higher than the 200-day MAP of $122.48, a second bullish indicator. The 200-day MAP has increased to $122.48, a third bullish indicator.

Past Quarter:

- In the last three months the stock has hit a new 52-week high twelve times, pointing to a significant uptrend.

2.2 Other Bullish Signals

- Return on Equity of 9.0% versus sector average of 5.8%.

MCap/Total Assets:

- Tobin's Q Ratio, defined as MCap divided by Total Assets, is 0.5. Compared with the rest of the market the stock is undervalued.

- Net profit margin has averaged 14.4% in the last 3 years. This is better than the sector average of 11.8% and suggests a high margin of safety.

- As per the Du Pont analysis, Return on Equity of 9.0% is better than sector average of 5.8%. This is computed as net profit margin of 15.2% times asset turnover [sales/assets] of 0.16 times leverage factor [total assets/shareholders' equity] of 3.6.

SECTION 3 ONGOING BULLISH PARAMETERS

3.1 Rule of 40

The stock scores a favorable score exceeding 40 when using the Rule of 40 (Revenue Growth plus EBITDA margin). Y.o.y revenue growth of 4%, EBITDA margin is 36.2% and the sum of the two 40.7% needs to exceed 40%.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

3.2 Created Market Value [CMV] past 17 yrs of $79.3 billion

- Market Capitalization has increased by $81.3 billion from $18.6 billion to $99.9 billion in the last 17 years. This increase comprises cumulative retained earnings (RETE) of $2 billion and Created Market Value of $79.3 billion. The Created Market Value multiple, defined by the change in MCap for every $1 of retained earnings is exemplary at $39.99.

3.3 Satisfies four criteria of Benjamin Graham

- The P/E of 22.5 is 0.4 times the highest average P/E of 57.7 in the last five years. This is a value criterion, according to Benjamin Graham who described as a value criterion "A P/E ratio down to less than four-tenth of the highest average P/E ratio the stock attained in the most recent five years".

-""Earnings growth over the most recent ten years of 7% compounded - that is a doubling of earnings in a five-year period""; the earnings per share have more than doubled in the last 5 years.

-""Stability of growth in earnings over the last 5 years, defined as no more than two declines of 5% or greater in year-end earnings""; there have been 2 declines in earnings in the last 5 years.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

3.4 Satisfies 5 out of 9 criteria of Joseph Piotroski [pass mark 5]:

- Positive net income.

- Positive operating cashflow.

- Return on Assets improvement [from 2.4% to 2.5%].

- Good quality of earnings [operating cashflow exceeds net income].

- Improvement in gross margin [from 98.0% to 98.1%].

But does not meet the following 4 criteria of Joseph Piotroski:

- Improvement in long-term debt to total assets.

- Improvement in current ratio.

- Total shares on issue unchanged (or reduction in total shares on issue).

- Improvement in asset turnover.

SECTION 5 PEER COMPARISON & RANKING OF DUK

5.1 LONG-TERM FUNDAMENTAL RANKING: 3 OUT OF 5 [5 is best]

Duke Energy Corporation is ranked number 1 out of 32 listed utility companies in the United States with a market capitalization of $99.9 billion.

Within its sector it has a relatively low P/E/G of 0.37.

It has a moderate relative ROE of 9.0% and ROA of 2.5%. The company paid a dividend of $3.19 in the last twelve months. The dividend yield is moderate at 3.3%. Finally, its earnings growth in the past 12 months has been a comparatively high 61.3%.

Stocks are scored on a set of parameters reflecting fundamental analytical tools involving valuation, size and financial performance. They are ranked according to the average values of those parameters. The highest ranking is 5 and the lowest ranking is 1.

Fri Apr 24, 2026: Duke Energy Secures 20-Year License for Robinson Nuclear Plant

Duke Energy Corporation has received a significant boost with the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission's (NRC) decision to grant a 20-year extension for the operating license of its Robinson Nuclear Plant. This renewal allows the facility to continue producing energy until the year 2050, a crucial development for the utility giant. The announcement was made on April 23rd, 2026, and signifies Duke Energy's commitment to providing stable and sustainable energy to the region.

The Robinson Nuclear Plant, located in Hartsville, South Carolina, plays a vital role in Duke Energy's operations, supplying electricity to approximately 570,000 households. This license extension not only secures the plant's operational future but also aligns with Duke Energy's broader strategy of maintaining a diverse energy portfolio. The company is focused on integrating nuclear, natural gas, and renewable energy sources to meet the growing energy demands of its 8 million customers across various states, including the Carolinas, Indiana, Florida, Ohio, and Kentucky.

Duke Energy operates within the regulated utilities sector, boasting a market capitalization of approximately $99.64 billion. The company is segmented into electric utilities and infrastructure, gas utilities and infrastructure, and commercial renewables. Currently, the company's P/E ratio stands at 20.26, which is close to its five-year median, indicating that the stock may be fairly valued in the current market context. Investors are encouraged to consider this valuation alongside other financial metrics when evaluating the stock's potential.

The GF Score(TradeMark) for Duke Energy is 77 out of 100, reflecting strong performance across key metrics that can lead to higher long-term returns. The company's profitability rank is notably strong at 7 out of 10, underscoring its operational efficiency. However, concerns regarding financial strength persist, with a rating of just 3 out of 10, suggesting potential issues related to debt levels and overall financial health. Investors should carefully weigh these factors when considering Duke Energy as part of their investment portfolio.

In recent months, insider trading activity has raised eyebrows, with insiders selling $5.6 million worth of shares and no recorded insider buying. This trend may lead to questions about insider confidence in the company's future performance. Overall, Duke Energy presents a mixed outlook for investors. While the recent license renewal for the Robinson Nuclear Plant strengthens its operational foundation, its financial challenges could impact long-term growth. Investors are encouraged to explore further insights about Duke Energy and its current market position.

Recent News 2 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 271 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Honored with Arbor Day Foundation’s Tree Line USA Award for 20th Year

Duke Energy Corporation has received national recognition for its tree care practices for the 20th consecutive year. The Arbor Day Foundation named Duke Energy Florida a 'Tree Line USA' utility, citing the company's rigorous tree pruning standards, comprehensive employee training, and public education efforts aimed at reducing power outages and safeguarding electrical infrastructure.

On 22nd April 2026, the Arbor Day Foundation lauded Duke Energy for meeting industry standards for tree care, supporting tree planting initiatives, and engaging in annual arbor education programs. The designation reflects Duke Energy's commitment to sustainable practices, as well as its proactive approach to maintaining the health of urban trees in proximity to electrical lines.

One of Duke Energy's ongoing initiatives includes an annual free tree giveaway for customers through the Arbor Day Foundation's Energy-Saving Trees program. In 2026, the company distributed 1,000 trees to Florida customers, bringing the total number of free trees given away statewide to over 16,000 since 2017. These trees are shipped directly to customers with detailed planting instructions designed to encourage proper placement away from power lines.

Company officials noted that proper tree placement is essential for conserving energy and minimizing the risk of outages during storm and hurricane seasons. Duke Energy also conducts year-round vegetation maintenance near power lines and ramps up these efforts ahead of hurricane season, ensuring a reliable power supply for its customers.

Additionally, Duke Energy reminds customers not to trim trees near power lines themselves and encourages them to contact Sunshine 811 before digging near underground utilities. More information about responsible tree planting near electrical equipment can be found through Duke Energy's Right Tree Right Place program.

Recent News 3 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 395 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Foundation Launches America250 Grants for South Carolina Green Spaces

GREENVILLE, S.C.– To strengthen communities across South Carolina as the nation approaches its 250th anniversary, Duke Energy Foundation is awarding $275,000 to nonprofit organizations through a first round of funding as part of its America250 initiative. This funding, announced on 22nd April 2026, celebrates Earth Day and aims to enhance local parks, tree planting efforts, and trail maintenance projects throughout the Palmetto State.

The grants, which form part of a total investment exceeding $550,000 in South Carolina, are aimed at revitalizing parks and shared green spaces, supporting tree planting, and litter prevention initiatives. "As we look back on our country's 250-year anniversary, it's an opportunity to reflect on our nation's history while working to support the next generation," said Tim Pearson, Duke Energy South Carolina president. "We are proud to partner with local organizations who can help care for the Palmetto State now, so future generations can enjoy its beauty."

Duke Energy Foundation has awarded a total of 11 grants in this first round of funding. Notable recipients include the South Carolina Wildlife Federation, Foothills Trail Conservancy, and the Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance. Each organization is expected to utilize the funding to enhance local environments and foster community engagement in conservation efforts. For instance, Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance plans to create a new trail that highlights the scenic beauty of Lake Keowee while educating visitors about the Revolutionary War history in the area.

The positive responses from grant recipients highlight the importance of such initiatives. Matt Flege, Executive Director of Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance, expressed gratitude for the support, stating, "The America250 grant will allow us to create a new trail showcasing the beauty of Lake Keowee to South Carolina residents and visitors alike." Similarly, Susan H. Wild, Chair of Sumter Green, emphasized how the investment strengthens their commitment to beautifying the community, ensuring that efforts made today will benefit future generations.

These grants mark the first awards under Duke Energy Foundation's America250 initiative, which represents a commitment of over $1 million towards community-driven projects across the company's six-state service territories. Additional grant recipients will be announced later this spring, including initiatives focused on veterans' workforce pathways and enhancing access to history and civics education. Duke Energy Foundation continues to provide nearly $30 million annually in philanthropic support to the communities it serves, reflecting its dedication to environmental stewardship and community development.

SECTION 2 TODAY'S BULLISH SIGNALS

2.1 Uptrend

Today's Volatility:

The 4% discount to 12-month high of $134.49 against the 20% premium to 12-month low of $107.42 suggests the stock continues to push for new highs.

Today its volatility (highest price minus lowest price/lowest price) of 1.6% equalled the average daily volatility, up from 1.2584091808468567% on Wednesday. Price/Moving Average Price of 1.05:

- The Price/MAP 200 for Duke Energy Corporation is 1.05. Being higher than 1 is a bullish indicator. The stock is trading above both its MAPs and the 50-day MAP of $133.03 is higher than the 200-day MAP of $122.48, a second bullish indicator. The 200-day MAP has increased to $122.48, a third bullish indicator.

Past Quarter:

- In the last three months the stock has hit a new 52-week high twelve times, pointing to a significant uptrend.

2.2 Other Bullish Signals

- Return on Equity of 9.0% versus sector average of 5.8%.

MCap/Total Assets:

- Tobin's Q Ratio, defined as MCap divided by Total Assets, is 0.5. Compared with the rest of the market the stock is undervalued.

- Net profit margin has averaged 14.4% in the last 3 years. This is better than the sector average of 11.8% and suggests a high margin of safety.

- As per the Du Pont analysis, Return on Equity of 9.0% is better than sector average of 5.8%. This is computed as net profit margin of 15.2% times asset turnover [sales/assets] of 0.16 times leverage factor [total assets/shareholders' equity] of 3.6.
